title: قرار الرئيس الاعلى للجامعة رقم 59 لسنة 2004: في شأن إنشاء برنامج ماجستير في علوم وهندسة البترول للحصول على درجات:1 – الماجستير في جيولوجيا البترول 2 – الماجستير في هندسة البترول 3 – الماجستير في الهندسة الكيميائية

provisions.title: ينشرهذا القرار فيالجريدةالرسمية.
provisions.content:
مكرر)من اللائحةالتنفيذيةلقانون إنشاءوتنظيمالجامعةبإضافةالفقرات 5 و6 و7إليها، لتصبحهذه المادةكما يلي:1–يمنح مجلسالجامعة بناءعلى اقتراحمجلس كليةالعلوم،وتوصية مجلسالشؤونالعلميةوالتعليمية،وبناء على عرضمدير الجامعةدرجةالماجستير فيعلوم البيئة،ويصدر بذلكقرار منالرئيسالاعلىللجامعة.2–يمنح مجلسالجامعة بناءعلى اقتراحعمادةالدراساتالعليابالجامعة،وتوصية مجلسالشؤونالعلميةوالتعليمية،وبناء على عرضمدير الجامعةدرجة ماجستيرالعلوم فيمواردالمياه،ويصدر بذلكقرار منالرئيسالاعلىللجامعة.3–يمنح مجلسالجامعة بناءعلى اقتراحعمادةالدراساتالعليابالجامعةوتوصية مجلسالشؤونالعلميةوالتعليمية،وبناء على عرضمدير الجامعةدرجةالماجستير فيعلوم وهندسةالمواد،ويصدر بذلكقرار منالرئيسالاعلىللجامعة.4–يمنح مجلسالجامعة بناءعلى اقتراحعمادةالدراساتالعليابالجامعةوتوصية مجلسالشؤونالعلميةوالتعليمية،وبناء على عرضمدير الجامعةدرجةالماجستير فيالاستشعار عنبعد ونظمالمعلوماتالجغرافية،ويصدر بذلكقرار منالرئيسالاعلىللجامعة.5–يمنح مجلسالجامعة بناءعلى اقتراحعمادةالدراساتالعليابالجامعةوتوصية مجلسالشؤونالعلميةوالتعليمية،وبناء على عرضمدير الجامعةدرجةالماجستير فيجيولوجياالبترول،ويصدر بذلكقرار منالرئيسالاعلىللجامعة.6- يمنح مجلسالجامعة بناءعلى اقتراحعمادةالدراساتالعليابالجامعةوتوصية مجلسالشؤونالعلميةوالتعليمية،وبناء على عرضمدير الجامعةدرجةالماجستير فيهندسةالبترول،ويصدر بذلكقرار منالرئيسالاعلىللجامعة.7–يمنح مجلسالجامعة بناءعلى اقتراحعمادةالدراساتالعليابالجامعةوتوصية مجلسالشؤونالعلميةوالتعليمية،وبناء على عرضمدير الجامعةدرجةالماجستير فيالهندسةالكيميائية،ويصدر بذلكقرار منالرئيسالاعلىللجامعة.المادة3ينشرهذا القرار فيالجريدةالرسمية.المادة4علىالجهاتالمعنيةتنفيذ هذاالقرار كلفيما يخصه.صدرفي 23/4/1425هـ.
الموافق12/6/2004م.نهيانمبارك آلنهيانالرئيسالاعلىللجامعةنشرهذا القرار فيعدد الجريدةالرسمية رقم416ص 689.UnitedArab Emirates UniversityDeanship of GraduateStudiesPetroleum Geosciences,Petroleum Engineering and Chemical EngineeringTobe offered by The Colleges of Sciences and EngineeringProposalDate: November 2003TargetStarting Date: September 2004Summaryof the M.Sc. Petroleum ProgramAdmissionrequirements:- B.Sc.Degree with a minimum cumulative grade point average (GPA) of 2.5 out of 4 inGeology, Petroleum Engineering, or Chemical Engineering respectively.-Applicants with B. Sc. degrees from other Engineering or Science branches with aGPA of 2,5 out of 4 or better may be also admitted. However, their admission isprovisional on completion of deficiency courses prior to or during their studyin the program.-Proficiency in English demonstrated by a score of 500 on the TOEFL test.Students scoring between 400-500 would be required to take 1-2 courses oftechnical English.In allcases, admission is processed on a competitive basis based on the candidate'sGPA, professional experience, and career objectives.Languageof Program:Englishis the language of instruction for the program.Modes ofenrollment:Studentsenroll either as part-time or full-time, with all classes held on weekends orafter 5 p. m. on weekdays.Degreesawarded:Successfulcandidates will be awarded, depending on. their study program, one of thefollowing degrees:M.Sc.Petroleum Geosciences - M.Sc. Petroleum Engineering - M.Sc. ChemicalEngineeringDegreerequirements:Successfulcompletion of either 31 credit hours (for the thesis option) or 34 credit hours(for the non-thesis option) is required, distributed as follows:ThesisOption:- Corecourses for all three disciplines (10 credit hours)-Specialized Core courses (9 credit hours)-Specialized Elective courses (6 credit hours)- MastersThesis (6 credit hours)Non-thesisoption:- Corecourses for all three disciplines (10 credit hours)-Specialized Core courses (9 credit hours)-Specialized Elective courses including an Independent Study (15 credithours)TheSpecialized Core and Elective courses cover the three areas of:-Petroleum Geosciences; Petroleum Engineering; Chemical EngineeringStudentsmay substitute I specialized elective with a course from the other twospecializations (Petroleum Geosciences, Petroleum Eng., or Chemical Eng.) orfrom another approved graduate level program with the approval of the programcommittee.Targetstarting date: September 2004.PrefaceThis isthe second version of the document prepared by the joint committee from theColleges of Engineering and Sciences for a coordinated Masters program leadingto M.Sc. degrees in the group of petroleum disciplines: petroleum geosciences,petroleum engineering, and chemical engineering. These disciplines are groupedtogether because of their relevancy and interdependency on each other, as thepetroleum geosciences discipline deals with exploration methods and describingsubsurface formations, the petroleum engineering discipline deals with thereservoir description, modeling, production and processing of petroleum, whilethe chemical engineering discipline covers crude oil processing, treatment,refining, and petrochemicals. In addition, most of the enhanced oil recoverytechniques are chemical processes. Collectively, these three disciplines make upthe core scientific proficiencies needed in the upstream and downstreampetroleum operations. As the challenges of industry demand the integration ofthe efforts of these disciplines, while also keeping the respectivespecializations, the programs of study at the university need also to reflectthis integration and specialization. This proposed program would allow itsstudents to tackle a number of challenging research issues that require thismultidisciplinary collaboration.Thisproposal had been preceded by two other proposals from the Chemical andPetroleum Engineering Department and the Geology Department for their respectiveMasters programs. Following the recommendation of the University's GraduateStudies Steering Committee and the approval of the DVC for Academic Affairs,these proposals have been combined into the current document; also, thisdocument has incorporated feedback from:*Petroleum and Chemical Industries, collected through two industry surveys.* Fourexpert international reviewers from leading U.S. Universities. These includedProf Colin Barker, Petroleum Geosciences - U. Tulsa, Prof. Larry Lake, PetroleumEngineering - U. Texas at Austin, Prof. Roland Horne- Chairman of PetroleumEngineering department, Stanford University, and Prof. Douglas R. Lloyd,Chemical Engineering - U. Texas at Austin.* And,the University Graduate Study Committee.The jointEngineering-Sciences committee preparing this program is comprised of:•Dr. Reyadh Almehaideb, Assoc. Professor of Petroleum Engineering, Associate Deanof Engineering.* Prof.Adel Othman, Prof. of Geophysics-Seismic Exploration, College of Sciences•Prof. Mohamed E. Osman, Professor of Petroleum Engineering, College ofEngineering.•Dr. Esam Abdelgawad - Assoc. Prof. of Petroleum Geochemistry, College ofSciences.•Dr. Fahmi Abu AI-Rub, Assoc. Professor of Chemical Engineering, College ofEngineering.Also, anumber of other faculty members from the three disciplines contributed theirinput, especially with regard to the course descriptions, for thisdocument.PART1: Definition of the Program1.1 INTRODUCTIONThedisciplines of Petroleum Geosciences, Petroleum Engineering, and ChemicalEngineering have special importance to the development of industry in generaland the petroleum and petrochemical industries in particular. The first two areessential for the upstream (production) side of the industry, while the thirdone provides the technical basis for the downstream (processing, refining, andpetrochemical) side of the Industry. The petroleum (crude oil and natural gas)industry provides almost 60% of the worldwide energy supplies. Technicalinnovations such as simulation of reservoir fluid flow, 3-D seismic, horizontaldrilling, deep-sea platforms, and enhanced oil recovery (EOR) processes, haveallowed us to extend the lifetime of the world's current oil reservoirs andrecover a larger proportion of the oil in the ground. They also allowed us toreach deep offshore fields and made marginal fields economical. Such innovationshave helped keep the reserves/production ratio almost constant for a century,and thus provided the world with a more effective, flexible, and cheap source ofenergy than any other source.Theprocessing and refining industry provides us with the different "cuts" ofpetroleum such as natural gas liquids (NGL), liquefied petroleum gas (LPG),gasoline, diesel fuel, naphtha, jet fuels, ...etc., that are used for differentpurposes. The catalytic cracking process has made it possible to extract lighterproducts from heavier crude oils. Chemical products like Methyl Tertiary ButylEther (MTBE) and Ethyl tertiary Butyl Ether (ETBE) have provided us with cleanerburning gasoline. The petrochemical industry with its hundreds of products suchas nylons, polyvinyl chloride (PVC), polyethylene, and composite materials havechanged the way we build our cars, planes, furniture, and even how we make ourclothes. The pesticide and fertilizer industries have had a major impact onagricultural production. The gas-liquefaction industry has made it possible totransport natural gas thousands of miles by sea, therefore providing cleanenergy to the consumers and a source of income for the producers.In theUnited Arab Emirates (UAE), the petroleum and petrochemical industries involvethe production, processing, refining of crude oil and natural gas and themanufacturing of chemical and petrochemical products' Revenues from thepetroleum industry constitute about two-thirds of the nation's budget, while thechemical and petrochemical industries provide about 70% of the value ofindustrial production. Between them, they provide over a third of the nation'sgross national product (GNP). These industries form and will continue to formthe backbone of economic activity in the country now and in the future, due totheir competitive advantage in the UAE relevant to a worldwide market Thisunique position for the industry in the country makes strong ties between therelevant departments of the University and the industrial sector (bothgovernmental. and private) an issue, that should be given high priority.The UAEUniversity has recently placed a large emphasis on research and graduate studiesrelevant to the different needs of the country. New interdisciplinarygraduate-level programs have been initiated .such as Environmental Science,Materials Science and Engineering, Water Resources, and the Master of BusinessAdministration (MBA). Also, proposals for new programs have been invited fromdifferent colleges and departments in the fields of education. Science,Engineering, Humanities, Business, Law, ... etc, and a few of these new programsare expected to start within the next three years. Funding of research, bothinternal and external, has also become more significant Moreover, the number ofbachelor-degree graduates is increasing steadily each year.The twocolleges of Sciences and Engineering at UAE University have gone through majorreorganizations and development since their inception about 25 years ago. TheCollege of Engineering has established new buildings comprised of offices anddepartmental lab facilities in 1990. The buildings contain over 40 labs, forboth general engineering and departments, 8 of which are for the Chemical andPetroleum Engineering Department, The Engineering College development culminatedin its undergraduate programs in Chemical and Petroleum along with the Civil,Electrical, Electrical (Communications), and Mechanical obtaining recognition as"Substantially Equivalent" International programs to their U.S. counterpartsfrom the Accreditation Board for Engineering and Technology (ABET) in 1998/99.The college of Sciences has also undergone a major laboratory modernizationeffort with new facilities established. The two colleges already collaborate inrunning the University's applied science M.Sc. programs in EnvironmentalSciences, Material Science & Engineering, and Water Resources.Withinthis framework, and with the anticipated need in the country to retrain and/oradvance the qualifications of specialists in the fields of petroleumgeosciences, petroleum engineering, and chemical engin
